itineraries.title: Budapest Region Itinerary: Danube Towns and Thermal Baths

visits.text: Discovery of the historic center of Szentendre, artists' village with colorful houses nicknamed the Hungarian Montmartre.
Visit to the Muveszet Malom, former sawmill transformed into a Hungarian contemporary art museum.
Discovery of the Belgrade Church, seat of the Orthodox bishopric, and its collection of Serbian icons.
Exploration of the Szentendre ecomuseum, open-air museum gathering more than 300 traditional Hungarian buildings.
Ascent to the citadel of Visegrád and panorama over the Danube bend.
Exploration of the medieval castle ruins and the historic center of the lower town.

activities.text: Walk along the Danube and discovery of the surrounding natural trails.

visits.text: Visit to the basilica of Esztergom, largest church in Hungary and symbol of national Christianity.
Discovery of the episcopal palace and the Franciscan cloister.
Walk along the Danube and exploration of the medieval caves and the Hegyes tower.
Visit to the baroque castle of Gödöllő, imperial residence of Sissi, restored after the Soviet period.

activities.text: Stroll through the royal gardens and discovery of the history of Empress Elisabeth.

visits.text: Discovery of Lake Hévíz, largest natural thermal lake in Europe, surrounded by giant water lilies.
Exploration of the thermal resorts of Lake Balaton and its surrounding baroque churches.

activities.text: Relaxing swim in the warm waters of Hévíz.

visits.text: Discovery of the historic center of Pécs, former European Capital of Culture.
Visit to Saint Peter's Cathedral and the Ottoman remains of the Gazi Kasim Pacha mosque.
Strolling through baroque alleys and local art galleries.
Visit to the vineyards of Tokaj, birthplace of Tokaji, the famous wine of kings.
Exploration of wine cellars and discovery of Hungarian oenological heritage.

activities.text: Tasting of local wines and discovery of the region's viticultural traditions.

seo.h1_title: Budapest Region Itinerary: Danube Towns and Thermal Baths
seo.intro_paragraph: This itinerary around Budapest explores Danube Bend towns, thermal spa destinations and historic cities. From Szentendre and Visegrád to Hévíz, Pécs and Tokaj, it reveals Hungary’s cultural and thermal heritage beyond the capital.
seo.seo_hook: Danube curves frame hilltop fortresses, warm waters steam year-round, and vineyards glow at dusk.

itineraries.title: Hungary Grand Tour: Thermal Baths, Lake Balaton and UNESCO Villages
itineraries.subtitle: From Budapest to Pécs via Eger, Lillafüred and Lake Balaton — eight days through baroque, Ottoman and thermal Hungary.
itineraries.notes: The Szántódrév–Tihanyrév ferry (D6) runs from April to October; outside this period, getting from Siófok to Tihany requires driving around the lake (approx. 90 km). The Eger minaret is only accessible from April to October. The Lillafüred forest railway operates mainly from May to September.

visits.text: Start at the 'Shoes on the Danube Bank' memorial (Cipők a Duna-parton), between the Chain Bridge and the Parliament. In 2005, artists Can Togay and Gyula Pauer fixed 60 pairs of metal shoes along 45 metres of riverbank — men's, women's, children's — to remember the hundreds of Jews forced to remove their shoes before being shot and thrown into the river by Arrow Cross militiamen in 1944–1945. The site is free and accessible at any hour. Pause for a moment: the simplicity of the installation makes the memorial all the more powerful.

The Hungarian Parliament is one of the most extravagant buildings in Central Europe. Built between 1885 and 1902 to the designs of Imre Steindl, it stretches 268 metres long, contains 691 rooms, 29 staircases and a dome rising to 96 metres — a height chosen to mark the millennium of the Hungarian nation in 1896. You can't miss it from the riverbank: its neo-Gothic façade lit up at night is Budapest's defining image. Book the interior tour in advance during high season — slots fill up fast.

St Stephen's Basilica (Szent István-bazilika) took more than 50 years to build — from 1851 to 1905 — and its dome measures exactly 96 metres, the same height as the Parliament: by law, no building was allowed to stand taller. Inside, look for the Chapel of the Holy Right, which houses the mummified right hand of King Stephen I, Hungary's foremost relic; a coin in the slot lights up the reliquary. Climb to the dome viewing platform via the lift or 364 steps for a 360° panorama over Budapest.

Vörösmarty Square and Váci Street, just next door, are the busy pedestrian arteries at the heart of Pest. Váci Street is a touch touristy but unmissable for the atmosphere and the shop fronts; walk all the way down to the Great Market Hall (Nagy Vásárcsarnok) at the southern end if you have time.

activities.text: Walk up Andrássy Avenue — a UNESCO World Heritage Site — to Heroes' Square (Hősök tere). This 2.3-kilometre boulevard was laid out in the late 19th century on the model of the Champs-Élysées. At the far end, the colossal statues of the seven Magyar tribal chieftains and Hungarian kings form an imposing tableau. Allow 45 minutes for the round-trip walk, longer if you duck into a museum.

End the day at the Széchenyi Thermal Baths (Széchenyi gyógyfürdő), inaugurated in 1913 inside a neo-Baroque palace in the Városliget park. These are Budapest's largest baths — 18 pools including three outdoor ones open even in winter — fed by an artesian spring at 74°C from more than 1,000 metres underground. Bring swimwear and a towel; book online in high season to skip the queue.

visits.text: Fő tér is the baroque heart of Szentendre, a small town perched on the Danube bank 20 kilometres from Budapest. In the centre stands the Plague Cross, erected in the 18th century by Serbian merchants to celebrate the town being spared by the epidemic. All around, colourfully painted houses recall the heritage of Serbian refugees who settled here from the 17th century onward, fleeing the Ottoman advance. Step inside the Blagovestenska Orthodox Church (18th century): its gilded icons are well worth a look.

The Margit Kovács Museum is one of Hungary's most visited: it brings together the work of this Hungarian ceramicist (1902–1977) whose sculptures blend folk art and symbolism with striking originality. Don't miss it if you have even the slightest interest in Hungarian craftsmanship. Right next door, some thirty galleries and artists' studios line the lanes — Szentendre has been an artists' colony since 1926.

A walk along the Danube embankment offers a different perspective on the town and the wooded hills across the water. If you have a half-day to spare, the Skanzen — an open-air museum on the outskirts — reconstructs a Hungarian village with 340 authentic period buildings and live craft demonstrations. Open from April to October.

activities.text: The most pleasant way to reach Szentendre from Budapest is by riverboat from April to October (around 1h30 from the Vigadó quay). Outside the season or to save time, the HÉV suburban rail line H5 from Batthyány tér gets you there in 40 minutes. The return by boat, with the lights of Budapest growing closer, is worth the trip in itself.

visits.text: Eger Castle commands the town from its hilltop and owes its fame to one of the most celebrated episodes in Hungarian history: in 1552, Captain István Dobó and roughly 2,000 defenders — women and children included — held out for 38 days against an Ottoman army estimated at 40,000. The victory became a national symbol, immortalised in Géza Gárdonyi's novel 'Eclipse of the Crescent Moon.' The Ottomans eventually took the town in 1596 and held it for 91 years. Allow two hours to explore the ramparts, the underground casemates and the exhibitions.

Dobó István Square is Eger's lively hub, lined with terraces, a statue of Captain Dobó and the 18th-century baroque Franciscan church. This is where the town comes alive in the evening — take a seat on a terrace and watch it unfold.

Eger's minaret is the only intact minaret in Hungary and the northernmost surviving Ottoman structure in Europe. Standing 40 metres tall and built in the early 17th century, you need to climb 97 steps up an extremely narrow spiral staircase to reach the top. The views over the town and the castle are worth the effort, but the ascent is not recommended for anyone with claustrophobia. Open from April to October.

Eight kilometres from Eger, the travertine terraces of Egerszalók form white limestone shelves shaped by thermal waters cascading down the hillside — a geological formation unique in Hungary. The view from the overlook is spectacular, especially in winter when steam rises into the cold air.

activities.text: In the late afternoon, head down to the Valley of the Beautiful Women (Szépasszony-völgy), two kilometres from the centre. More than 200 wine cellars carved into the volcanic tuff line a sun-drenched south-facing slope — ideal conditions for ageing wine. This is where you'll taste Egri Bikavér, 'Bull's Blood,' a full-bodied red blend named after a legend: the defenders of 1552 supposedly fought with such ferocity after drinking this wine that the Ottomans believed they'd been fortified by actual bull's blood. Allow an hour for a leisurely tasting in one of the cellars.

visits.text: The Szinva waterfall is Hungary's tallest man-made cascade — 20 metres — created in the 1920s during the construction of the Palace Hotel, when the stream was diverted at the initiative of Count István Bethlen. Artificial or not, it's beautiful: in summer the water plunges through terraced gardens planted with rare species; in winter it freezes into spectacular columns of ice. Walk down to the hanging gardens for the closest view.

Lake Hámori stretches 1.5 kilometres and was formed in the early 19th century when the Szinva and Garadna streams were dammed to supply the nearby blast furnaces. Today it's a perfect spot for a stroll: the trail along the northern shore loops around in about 45 minutes, with the neo-Renaissance Palace Hotel (built between 1927 and 1930) reflected in the water as a focal point. In summer, pedal boats can be hired on the spot.

Don't leave without a look at Anna Cave and St Stephen's Cave, both within walking distance of the hotel. The first is a 1,043-metre-long stalactite cave of which visitors see only a portion; the second lies 500 metres along the road toward Eger.

activities.text: The Lillafüred narrow-gauge forest railway (Lillafüredi Állami Erdei Vasút) is one of Hungary's finest scenic lines. Built in the 1920s to haul timber from the Bükk Mountains, it now runs from Miskolc to Garadna through beech forests, gorges, bridges and tunnels. The wooden carriages are open on the sides — bring a jacket. In service mainly from May to September; check timetables beforehand.

practical_info.booking: Tickets at the station — check timetables in advance

visits.text: Make a stop in Székesfehérvár, Hungary's former royal capital where 37 kings were crowned — and 17 buried — before the Ottoman invasion. The pedestrianised centre holds the Garden of Ruins (Romkert), where the apse and a few pillars of King Stephen I's medieval basilica, founder of the kingdom, still stand. Don't miss the fresco by Vilmos Aba-Novák that depicts the history of Hungary in a small neighbouring chapel.

Bory Castle (Bory-vár) is one of Hungary's most eccentric curiosities: architect and sculptor Jenő Bory (1879–1959) built it single-handedly over 40 consecutive summers starting in 1923 — with no formal plans, guided only by his imagination and his love for his painter wife Ilona Komócsin. Seven towers, thirty rooms, courtyards and galleries rise from quartz concrete in a wild mix of Romanesque, Gothic and Scottish styles. The building entered the Guinness Book of Records as the largest structure ever erected by one person. Allow an hour to lose yourself in it.

In Siófok, the 45-metre Water Tower (Víztorony) is the symbol of the town. Designed in 1912 by architects Árpád Guth and Jenő Gergely, it now houses a viewing gallery and the tourist office. Climb up for views over Lake Balaton and the marina.

activities.text: In the evening, head to the Petőfi sétány promenade along the lake's biggest beach, the Nagystrand. Siófok is Hungary's 'summer capital' — buzzing, festive, with a well-known nightlife scene. Jókai Park (4.6 hectares), with its Japanese garden and pond, is the perfect spot for a quiet stroll before dinner.

visits.text: Take the ferry from Szántódrév (south shore, Siófok side) to Tihanyrév (north shore): ten minutes on the water to reach the Tihany peninsula where the lake narrows to just 1.4 kilometres. Ferries run frequently from April to October; outside the season, plan to drive around. Views of the hills from the deck.

Tihany's Benedictine Abbey was founded in 1055 by King Andrew I of Hungary and is one of the most historically significant sites in the country. Its Romanesque crypt houses the only medieval royal tomb still in situ in Hungary. The current twin-towered church was rebuilt between 1719 and 1754 in the baroque style; inside, the carved woodwork and frescoes by Lajos Deák Ébner reward a lingering look. From the forecourt, the view over Lake Balaton is the finest on the entire peninsula.

Echo Hill (Visszhang-domb, Pisky promenade) has been famous since the 18th century for its peculiar acoustics: depending on atmospheric conditions, a voice could once reverberate up to 15 times. Celebrated Hungarian poems reference this echo. A statue marks the spot of best resonance.

The lanes of Tihany village, with their thatched-roof houses and lavender shops, are worth a wander. The Lavender House tells the story of lavender cultivation on the peninsula (introduced in the early 20th century) and its unique volcanic geology. Try the lavender ice cream — it's the essential local treat.

activities.text: The coastal trail circling the peninsula takes two to three hours on foot and delivers constantly changing views of the lake. Tihany was Hungary's first protected nature reserve (designated in 1952); its two inland lakes attract numerous rare bird species. Walking shoes recommended — some sections are steep.

map_keywords: Pécsi Székesegyház Szent Péter és Pál

visits.text: On Széchenyi tér, the main square of Pécs, you'll find one of the best-preserved Ottoman monuments in Europe: the Mosque of Pasha Gazi Qasim, built between 1543 and 1546 using materials salvaged from demolished medieval churches. After the Habsburg reconquest in 1686, the minaret was torn down and the building was converted into a Catholic church. Step inside: the interior layers an Ottoman dome with Christian frescoes on the same walls, with verses from the Quran still visible. One building, two centuries of faith.

The Zsolnay Cultural Quarter grew out of the conversion of the former Zsolnay ceramics factory, founded in 1853 and world-renowned for its iridescent eosin glazes — a technology patented in 1893. Those golden-green ceramics still adorn numerous Art Nouveau buildings in Budapest and across Europe. The Zsolnay Museum and galleries display exceptional pieces from the early 20th century. The quarter also hosts a light festival in summer (Zsolnay Light Festival).

The Cathedral of Saints Peter and Paul in Pécs is one of Hungary's most important Romanesque monuments. The original crypt dates from the 11th and 12th centuries; the building was heavily remodelled in the baroque era. Just below it, the Early Christian Necropolis of Sopianae has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2000: 4th-century mausolea with frescoes of the Virgin and of Adam and Eve lie buried beneath the modern streets.

Climb to the Tettye quarter, on the heights above Pécs, to see the ruins of the Ottoman episcopal palace and enjoy a fine viewpoint over the city. The surrounding green spaces are a local favourite at weekends.

If you still have energy, the Pécs TV Tower (Misina antenna, 534 m altitude) offers the widest panorama in the region — all the way to the southern plains and the Villány vineyards. Reachable on foot from the centre in 45 minutes or by road.

activities.text: Pécs has one main pedestrian street, Király utca, packed with cafés, restaurants and shops. Stroll along it in the late afternoon, have a glass of Villány wine — the neighbouring wine region produces some of Hungary's finest reds — and finish at Lake Balokány and its green spaces as the sun goes down.

visits.text: The Fisherman's Bastion (Halászbástya) is Budapest's most photographed monument — and with good reason: its seven neo-Romanesque turrets, built between 1895 and 1902 by architect Frigyes Schulek, frame the most spectacular panorama over the Danube, the Parliament and the rooftops of Pest. The seven towers represent the seven Magyar tribes who founded Hungary in the 9th century. Arrive early in the morning to beat the crowds; at night, access to the terraces is often free. Matthias Church just behind, with its multicoloured tiled roof, was the coronation site of several Hungarian kings — including Franz Joseph I in 1867.

The Buda Castle Quarter (Várnegyed) surrounds the rebuilt Royal Palace, home to the Hungarian National Gallery and the Budapest History Museum. The esplanades and medieval lanes are worth an hour's wander: the views across to Pest from the eastern ramparts are consistently stunning.

Margaret Island (Margit-sziget) takes its name from the daughter of King Béla IV, Margaret, who lived in a Dominican convent on the island until her death in 1271. Stretching 2.5 kilometres long and 500 metres wide in the heart of the Danube, it packs in rose gardens, Japanese gardens, medieval ruins and the musical dancing fountain — a free show every hour with synchronised water jets. Perfect for a relaxed stroll at the end of the trip.

activities.text: Round off the trip with a twilight cruise on the Danube (1 hour). As the current carries you along, the illuminated Parliament, the Fisherman's Bastion, the historic bridges and Buda Castle will glide past in golden light — the sequence of monuments that earned the site its UNESCO listing. Several companies depart from the Vigadó quay on the Pest side; the evening departures are often the most beautiful.

seo.h1_title: Hungary Grand Tour: Thermal Baths, Lake Balaton and UNESCO Villages
seo.intro_paragraph: Budapest and its neo-Baroque thermal baths, Eger and its castle that held off 40,000 Ottomans, Lillafüred with its waterfall and beech forests, Lake Balaton and the mystical Tihany peninsula, then Pécs and its mosques turned churches: this grand tour spans eight centuries of Hungarian history in eight days. Every stop layers cultures — Roman, Ottoman, baroque, Art Nouveau — in a country that has been soothing travellers in thermal waters since antiquity.
seo.seo_hook: Century-old thermal baths, fortresses that defied the Ottomans, a millennial peninsula on Lake Balaton and baroque mosques: Hungary in eight days.
featured_list: Budapest: a 268-metre Parliament and the Széchenyi Baths, open since 1913
Eger: the fortress that repelled 40,000 Ottomans in 1552 and the Bull's Blood wine
Lake Balaton: a Benedictine abbey founded in 1055 and a ferry crossing to the Tihany peninsula
Pécs: a 16th-century Ottoman mosque converted into a church and UNESCO-listed Zsolnay ceramics

places_taglines.HU::budapest: A Danube capital where neo-Gothic grandeur and century-old thermal waters meet under UNESCO skies
places_taglines.HU::eger: A baroque hillside town famed for its heroic fortress and its cellars of Bull's Blood wine
places_taglines.HU::lillafured: A mountain retreat in the Bükk, where a waterfall, a mirror lake and a forest railway weave through beech groves
places_taglines.HU::siofok: The sun-soaked summer capital on Lake Balaton's southern shore
places_taglines.HU::pecs: Where a Roman necropolis, an Ottoman mosque and Art Nouveau ceramics share the same sunlit streets

highlights: Budapest's Parliament, built between 1885 and 1902, is one of the three largest parliament buildings in the world, with 691 rooms and a 96-metre dome
Eger Castle withstood a 38-day siege in 1552 against an Ottoman army 20 times its size — the most celebrated episode in Hungarian history
Tihany Abbey, founded in 1055, contains the only medieval royal tomb still in its original location in Hungary: that of King Andrew I
The Pasha Qasim Mosque in Pécs, built between 1543 and 1546, is the largest surviving Ottoman structure in Hungary, now converted into a Catholic church

itineraries.title: Hungary Road Trip: Thermal Baths, Lake Balaton and Budapest
itineraries.subtitle: From the Austrian border to the vineyards of Tokaj — a loop through Hungary's thermal springs, medieval castles and baroque splendour.

visits.text: Start by climbing the Fire Tower (Tűztorony) — its Romanesque foundations date from the 13th century, but the baroque silhouette you see today was rebuilt after a major fire in 1676. More than 100 spiral steps take you 60 metres up, where the view over the old town rooftops and the Austrian hills is striking. The Fire Tower owes its name to its role as a fire-watch post — an ironic function, given that it burned down itself.

Fő tér is Sopron's heart: the Holy Trinity Column stands at the centre, surrounded by immaculate baroque façades. Look closely at the gate at the foot of the tower — it's called the Gate of Loyalty, erected to commemorate the December 1921 referendum in which the townspeople voted 65% to remain in Hungary rather than be annexed to Austria after the Treaty of Trianon. This small border town has borne the title civitas fidelissima ever since.

Step inside the Goat Church (Kecske-templom), a 13th-century Gothic building whose name comes from a local legend: a goat supposedly unearthed a treasure that funded its construction. In the 18th century, this baroque church served as a coronation venue and meeting place for the Hungarian Parliament — making it one of the most historically significant churches in the country. The 18th-century rococo wooden pulpit deserves a closer look.

Detour into Új utca (New Street) to see the medieval synagogue built in the 14th century by Sopron's Jewish community — one of the oldest in Hungary. The inner courtyard contains a restored mikveh (ritual bath). After the expulsion of 1526 the building was converted into a dwelling, then rehabilitated from the 1960s onward.

activities.text: Take your time strolling the Várkörút, the circular boulevard that traces the old medieval ramparts of Sopron. Walking along these partially preserved walls, you'll understand why the town held out against the Ottomans while Buda and Pécs fell — it was never occupied. The Lővérek forest, accessible on foot from the centre, offers shaded trails and a few lookout points over the Hungarian plain.

visits.text: The thermal lake at Hévíz is simply one of a kind: at 4.4 hectares, it's the world's largest biologically active natural hot-water lake. The water surges from a spring 40 metres below at a rate of 410 litres per second and a temperature of 40°C — which is why the entire lake renews itself every 28 to 40 hours. In winter the water rarely drops below 24°C, and in summer it climbs to 36°C. You'll swim surrounded by pink water lilies imported in the late 19th century, while steam rising off the surface gives the place an almost otherworldly feel. Consider renting a float ring at the entrance.

Five kilometres away, Festetics Castle in Keszthely is the third-largest castle in Hungary. Construction began in 1745 under Kristóf Festetics, and the baroque building was expanded several times through the 18th and 19th centuries to reach 101 rooms. It now houses the largest intact aristocratic library in Europe: 86,000 volumes that survived the looting of the Second World War. The grounds and the former tropical glasshouse are worth a look.

activities.text: The real Hévíz experience is the swim: walk down the few steps from the thermal complex terraces and let yourself float in the mildly sulphurous water, rich in calcium, magnesium and radon. The sulphur smell is there but subtle. Allow at least two hours to make the most of it — regulars spend the entire morning reading newspapers propped on their floats. Avoid summer weekends, when the site gets very crowded.

visits.text: Sümeg Castle perches on an isolated hilltop commanding the plain — and that's no accident: King Béla IV had it built in the 1260s precisely for this reason, in the wake of the Mongol invasion of 1241. The fortress, 125 metres long by 80 metres wide, was never taken by the Turks, even though they occupied the town below on several occasions. Permanent exhibitions recreate the medieval dungeons, the chapel and the blacksmith's workshop. In summer, jousting tournaments are staged in the courtyard.

At the foot of the castle hill, the 18th-century baroque Episcopal Palace is a monument often overlooked by visitors. Built by Bishop Padányi Bíró Márton in the mid-18th century — when the Veszprém diocese had retreated to Sümeg in the face of the Turks — it illustrates the town's baroque heyday. Its fresco-decorated halls rank among the finest north of Lake Balaton.

activities.text: From the top of the castle, the panoramic view over the Transdanubian plain, the ancient volcanic cones and the distant Bakony hills is one of the finest in the region. The trail circling the ramparts takes about 30 minutes. From Sümeg, a 20-kilometre detour to Tapolca lets you visit the underground cave lake — a natural curiosity unique in Europe, explored by boat.

visits.text: Balatonfüred is the oldest spa resort on Lake Balaton. Walk along the Tagore sétány, the lakeside promenade — named after the Indian poet Rabindranath Tagore, who came here for a cardiac cure. The historic spa centre was already renowned in the 19th century for treating heart conditions, and a cardiac hospital still operates here. The marina, neoclassical villas and lakeside terraces make this the most elegant stop on the northern shore.

The Tihany peninsula juts into Lake Balaton like a finger from the northern shore. At the top of an 80-metre hill, the Benedictine abbey was founded in 1055 by King Andrew I — he is buried here, in the Romanesque crypt that is one of the oldest in Hungary. The present baroque church, built between 1719 and 1754, dominates the village with its twin towers. From the forecourt, the view over the lake is the best on the entire Balaton. In June, the surrounding hills turn into a carpet of purple as the lavender fields bloom — lavender ice cream in the village is a must.

activities.text: Echo Hill (Visszhang-domb), just below the abbey, has been known for centuries for its acoustics: sounds bounce off the church walls and return amplified. The Tihany peninsula is also Hungary's first protected nature reserve (designated in 1952) — marked trails cross its two crater lakes, the Belső-tó and Külső-tó, which sit at a higher elevation than Lake Balaton itself.

If you have time on the road between Sümeg and Balatonfüred, make a stop at Herend, 45 kilometres north, to visit the porcelain manufactory founded in 1826 — its hand-painting workshops are open to visitors. Alternatively, Ajka (crystal) is in the same direction. It's not on the direct route, but this is one of the only manufactories of its kind still operating in Central Europe.

visits.text: The Fisherman's Bastion (Halászbástya) may be Budapest's most photographed monument — and yet it's much younger than its medieval appearance suggests: built between 1895 and 1902 by architect Frigyes Schulek to mark the millennium of the Hungarian state, it never served any defensive purpose whatsoever. Its seven conical turrets represent the seven Magyar tribes who founded Hungary in 895. Access to the terraces is free before 9 AM and after 9 PM — arrive early to beat the crowds and enjoy the view of the illuminated Parliament. Right next door, the 14th-century Gothic Matthias Church, where Hungarian kings were crowned, deserves 30 minutes inside for its neo-Gothic frescoes.

On the Pest bank of the Danube, the Hungarian Parliament is the third-largest parliament building in the world — 268 metres long, 691 rooms and 16 tonnes of gold in its decoration. Built between 1884 and 1902 in a spectacular neo-Gothic style, it now houses the Crown of Saint Stephen behind bulletproof glass. Book your guided tour well in advance — tickets sell out fast in high season. The Danube embankment walk around the building, a UNESCO World Heritage Site, is free.

In Pest's Jewish quarter, the Great Synagogue on Dohány Street is the largest in Europe and the second-largest in the world. Inaugurated in 1859, it seats 3,000 worshippers. In its garden stands the Tree of Life, a metal sculpture in the shape of a weeping willow whose every leaf bears the name of a Hungarian Jew murdered during the Holocaust. Don't miss the surrounding lanes of the Jewish quarter with their hidden courtyards and ruin bars tucked into former tenement buildings.

The Széchenyi Baths were the first thermal baths built on the Pest side, inaugurated in 1913 in a striking neo-Baroque style. The water rises at 76°C from an artesian well 1,246 metres deep — it's cooled before feeding the 15 indoor pools and 3 outdoor pools open year-round. The classic scene: elderly gentlemen playing chess on floating boards in the 36°C outdoor pool. Book online to skip the queue.

activities.text: Board a twilight cruise on the Danube — this is where the city reveals its true grandeur, especially once night falls and the monuments light up. Operators offer one- to two-hour cruises from the central Pest embankment. The simultaneous view of the Parliament, Buda Castle, the seven bridges and the Fisherman's Bastion is worth the ticket alone.

Explore Andrássy Avenue and its 19th-century covered passages — the finest is the Párisi Udvar (Paris Court), built in 1909 in a blend of Art Nouveau and Gothic styles. This UNESCO-listed avenue leads all the way to Heroes' Square and Városliget park. In the evening, the ruin bars of the 7th District — abandoned tenement courtyards turned into bars — offer a deep dive into Budapest's nightlife.

visits.text: Szentendre, the 'artists' town,' sits 25 kilometres north of Budapest on the Danube. Its historic heart, Fő tér, is dominated by a marble Plague Cross erected in the 18th century by Serbian merchants to celebrate the town being spared by the epidemic. The colourful baroque houses date from the arrival of Serbian settlers in the 17th and 18th centuries — families fleeing the Ottoman occupation of Serbia, which explains the many Orthodox churches you'll come across in the lanes.

The Margit Kovács Museum showcases the work of Hungary's most famous 20th-century ceramicist, whose naïve, poetic style blends peasant influences with Art Deco. It's one of the most visited museums in Hungary outside Budapest — allow 45 minutes. Szentendre has more than 30 galleries and museums for a population of roughly 25,000, a concentration of art that is probably unique in Europe.

On the outskirts of town, the open-air museum (Skanzen) recreates Hungarian rural life from every region of the country across several hectares. Entire houses have been dismantled, moved and rebuilt, complete with period furniture, tools and farm animals — including the grey Hungarian cattle and the woolly Mangalica pig, two breeds typical of the puszta. Allow at least two hours to see it all.

activities.text: From Szentendre, a stop at Visegrád (13 km north) is highly recommended if you have time: the hilltop fortress commands one of the most spectacular views of the Great Danube Bend (Dunakanyar) — where the river makes a sharp 90° turn between forested hills. Once a royal residence, Visegrád's upper castle served as headquarters for Hungarian kings in the 14th century.

visits.text: Hollókő is the only village in Hungary inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list, since 1987 — and the designation is well deserved. The name means 'Raven Stone' in Hungarian, from a legend in which ravens dismantled a castle stone by stone to free a captive woman. The old village, spread over 145 hectares, contains 55 protected buildings in the Palóc architectural style of the 17th and 18th centuries: whitewashed walls, wooden porches, tiled roofs dating from the reconstruction after the last major fire in 1909. Around 400 people still live here and maintain the traditions — at Easter, they dress in their colourful folk costumes.

The ruins of Hollókő Castle stand at 365 metres on Mount Szár, above the village. Built in the 13th century by the noble Kacsics clan after the Mongol invasion, it played a defensive role during the Ottoman wars before being finally abandoned in 1683 at the end of the Turkish occupation. The castle has been partially restored, and its 360° panorama over the Cserhát hills is the reward for the 15-minute climb from the village.

activities.text: Hollókő's single-street village is best explored slowly, on foot, stopping in the small artisan workshops — leatherworkers, woodturners, potters — that occupy the historic houses. The village museum faithfully recreates an early 20th-century Palóc interior with its everyday and decorative objects. A short hike into the surrounding Cserhát hills offers perspectives over a landscape of orchards, vineyards and meadows that has barely changed in two centuries.

visits.text: Eger's Basilica, built in a neoclassical style between 1831 and 1836 by architect József Hild, is the second-largest church in Hungary after the one in Esztergom. Its six 17-metre Corinthian columns and sculpted tympanum dominate the central square. The interior seats 6,000 and its remarkable acoustics explain the regular organ concerts held here. Beneath the church lies a network of cellars — 'the city under the city' — visitable with a separate ticket.

Eger Castle (Egri Vár) is one of the defining sites of Hungarian resistance. In September 1552, Captain István Dobó and 2,000 soldiers held out for 38 days against a besieging Ottoman army of 40,000 — a victory that passed into national legend. The heroic defence was famously attributed to the local red wine, which the warriors smeared across their beards to appear more ferocious, giving rise to the name Egri Bikavér, 'Bull's Blood.' The castle fell nonetheless in 1596, ushering in 91 years of Turkish occupation. The castle museums and underground passages are not to be missed.

Don't miss Eger's Turkish minaret, the only intact minaret in Hungary. Standing 40 metres tall and built in the early 17th century, it rises in the heart of the old town as a remnant of the 91 years of Ottoman rule (1596–1687). Its 97 steps lead to a balcony with a distinctive view over baroque Eger. Open from April to October only.

activities.text: The Valley of the Beautiful Women (Szépasszony-völgy) lies one kilometre from central Eger: more than 200 wine cellars carved into the volcanic tuff line up over a few hundred metres. This is where you'll taste Egri Bikavér, Eger's robust red and one of Hungary's most famous wines. A network of 96 kilometres of underground tunnels runs beneath the town, connecting some of these cellars. Allow at least an hour for a proper tasting.

Eger's thermal baths combine indoor and outdoor pools fed by waters ranging from 28 to 68°C. The Turkish baths (Török fürdő) date from the Ottoman occupation — Arnaut Pasha built the first hammam here in the 16th century. A perfect end-of-day wellness stop after the castle.

visits.text: Tokaj sits at the confluence of two rivers — the Tisza and the Bodrog — and it's precisely this confluence that creates the autumn mists essential for the development of noble rot (Botrytis cinerea) on the Furmint and Hárslevelű grapes. That's why Tokaj wine, whose documented production dates back to 1571, can only come from here and nowhere else. Louis XIV, who adored it, proclaimed it 'vinum regum, rex vinorum' — the wine of kings, the king of wines — and Tsar Peter the Great kept a permanent Russian garrison in Tokaj charged with supplying the imperial court. UNESCO inscribed the Tokaj-Hegyalja cultural landscape in 2002.

The Tokaj Wine Museum holds autographs from Louis XIV and Tsar Peter the Great attesting to their fascination with the wine, along with a room dedicated to the 1772 vineyard classification system — one of the earliest in the world. It also gives access to a 16th-century cellar where Hungarian oak barrels (the 136-litre gönci, the standard unit of measure for Tokaji) still age. The guided tour lasts about 45 minutes.

activities.text: Climb Mount Tokaj (Kopaszhegy, 512 metres) — a moderate 45-minute hike through UNESCO-listed terraced vineyards. At the summit, a 25-metre wooden observation tower inaugurated in 2017 offers a 360° panorama over the Tisza–Bodrog confluence, the patchwork of vines and the Zemplén hills. This is the vantage point from which you'll understand why the region earned its UNESCO listing.

Operators offer tastings in the region's historic cellars — some date back to the 15th century, hewn into volcanic rock. Book ahead in September–October (harvest season), when the estates are at their busiest. The region has more than 3,000 wine cellars in total. The speciality to ask for first: Tokaji Aszú, a luscious sweet wine made from individually hand-picked overripe berries.

practical_info.booking: Booking recommended, especially during harvest season (September–November)

seo.h1_title: Hungary Road Trip: Thermal Baths, Lake Balaton and Budapest
seo.intro_paragraph: From Sopron, the civitas fidelissima clinging to the Austrian border, to the UNESCO-listed vineyards of Tokaj, this circuit crosses Hungary from west to east following the country's two great passions: thermal waters and medieval history. A castle never taken by the Ottomans, a thermal lake with pink water lilies, a Benedictine abbey founded in 1055, a baroque capital and a timeless Palóc village — every stop has its own chapter of history.
seo.seo_hook: The world's largest thermal lake, castles that defied the Ottomans, Budapest at twilight from the Danube, and the wine of kings in its 16th-century cellars.
featured_list: Hévíz: the world's largest natural thermal lake, open year-round with water at 24–36°C
Budapest: the 268-metre Parliament, the 1913 Széchenyi Baths and Europe's largest synagogue
Eger: 2,000 Hungarian soldiers who held out 38 days against 40,000 Ottomans in 1552
Tokaj: the wine of kings, UNESCO-listed since 2002, produced since the 16th century in hand-dug cellars

places_taglines.HU::sopron: A medieval border town that voted to stay Hungarian in 1921, earning the title civitas fidelissima
places_taglines.HU::heviz: The world's largest natural thermal lake — 4.4 hectares of warm water renewed every 40 hours
places_taglines.HU::sumeg: A 13th-century hilltop fortress the Turks never conquered, commanding the Transdanubian plain
places_taglines.HU::balatonfured: Lake Balaton's oldest spa resort and the gateway to Tihany's 1055 Benedictine abbey
places_taglines.HU::budapest: A Danube capital of thermal waters, where the world's third-largest parliament meets the 1913 Széchenyi Baths
places_taglines.HU::szentendre: A baroque artists' colony founded by Serbian refugees, with over 30 galleries for 25,000 inhabitants
places_taglines.HU::holloko: Hungary's only UNESCO village, frozen in its 1911 state with 55 whitewashed Palóc houses
places_taglines.HU::eger: A baroque town famed for its heroic 1552 stand against the Ottomans and its cellars of Bull's Blood wine
places_taglines.HU::tokaj: Capital of a UNESCO sweet wine proclaimed the wine of kings by Louis XIV

highlights: Hévíz: the world's only natural thermal lake open year-round, ideal even in winter thanks to a minimum water temperature of 24°C
Eger: the 1552 Ottoman siege is commemorated every summer with historical jousts in the castle courtyard
Tokaj: harvest season (September–November) is the best time to visit the cellars and watch the aszú grapes being picked
Budapest: book Parliament tickets and Széchenyi Baths massage slots several days ahead in high season

itineraries.title: What to See in Budapest: Architecture and Heritage
itineraries.subtitle: Jewish memory, baroque palaces, Art Nouveau thermal baths and royal panoramas on the Danube — Budapest in 19 stops.

visits.text: Shoes on the Danube Bank memorial

activities.text: Don't walk past this memorial without stopping. Along roughly 45 metres of riverbank between the Chain Bridge and the Parliament, 60 pairs of cast-iron shoes are set into the stone, facing the water. This is where the fascist Arrow Cross Party executed hundreds of Jews between December 1944 and January 1945: before shooting them and throwing them into the Danube, the militiamen forced them to remove their shoes — footwear was a valuable commodity in wartime. Filmmaker Can Togay and sculptor Gyula Pauer conceived this memorial in 2005, inaugurated on 16 April, ahead of the 60th anniversary of the victory over fascism. The 60 pairs represent carefully chosen 1940s styles: men's boots, women's heels, children's small shoes. Nobody was spared. Three cast-iron plaques inscribed in Hungarian, English and Hebrew record the facts. Take tram 2 or 2A to get here. Allow yourself time: the stone bench behind the sculptures was designed for contemplation.

visits.text: Great Market Hall (Nagyvásárcsarnok)

activities.text: From the moment you step inside, you'll see why this building is sometimes mistaken for a railway station: the iron interior framework, the tall glass windows and the sheer dimensions — 10,000 m² across three floors — are truly spectacular. Architect Samu Pecz inaugurated the hall on 15 February 1897, and the roof was clad in the famous colourful ceramics from the Zsolnay manufactory in Pécs, the same tiles found on Matthias Church. The building was commissioned by Budapest's first mayor, Károly Kamermayer, to address the sanitary problems of food supply in a fast-growing metropolis. During the Second World War the hall was damaged, then declared derelict in 1991 before being restored and reopened in 1994. Arrive early in the morning — the produce is fresher and the tourists fewer. Ground floor: quality Hungarian paprika, charcuterie, cheese, honey and the essential unicum. Upper floor: crispy lángos (fried dough with sour cream and cheese) and souvenir stalls. Bring cash — many vendors don't accept cards.

map_keywords: Az élet fája szobor Budapest Nagy Zsinagóga

visits.text: Memorial Garden (Raoul Wallenberg Park)

activities.text: Behind the Great Synagogue lies one of Budapest's most moving memorials. In this park — once part of the Budapest Jewish Ghetto where roughly 70,000 people were confined in autumn 1944 — stands the Tree of Life, a weeping willow in metal created by Hungarian sculptor Imre Varga and unveiled in 1991. Some also see the shape of an inverted menorah. On every steel leaf is engraved the name of a Jewish victim of the Holocaust; families still come to have the names of lost relatives added. The monument was partly funded by American actor Tony Curtis, whose parents were Hungarian. The park also pays tribute to Raoul Wallenberg, the Swedish diplomat who saved thousands of Hungarian Jews by issuing them diplomatic passports, as well as to other Righteous Among the Nations. More than 2,000 people who died in the ghetto were hastily buried in this garden during the winter of 1944–45, before Soviet forces liberated Budapest in February 1945.

visits.text: Budapest's Jewish Quarter

activities.text: The 7th District, between Dohány Street and Kazinczy Street, is one of Budapest's most fascinating and contradictory neighbourhoods. From the second half of the 19th century, many Jews here Magyarised their names as a sign of integration. Today, crumbling inner courtyards sit alongside ruin bars that have become famous across Europe, and restored synagogues stand between former Art Nouveau tenement blocks. The quarter is defined by a 'triangle of synagogues' representing the three branches of modern Judaism: the Great Synagogue (Neolog), the Rumbach Street synagogue (Reformed) and the Kazinczy Street synagogue (Orthodox). Wander the lanes at random — some courtyards reveal frescoes, old pharmacies or remarkable wrought-iron staircases. In the evening, the quarter comes alive and ruin-bar terraces spill into courtyards that haven't been renovated since the 1940s.

visits.text: Great Synagogue on Dohány Street

activities.text: This is the largest synagogue in Europe and the second-largest in the world after Temple Emanu-El in New York: 75 metres long, 27 metres wide, two 44-metre towers, seating for 3,500. It was built between 1854 and 1859 by Viennese architect Ludwig Förster in a Moorish style inspired by the Alhambra, with much of its interior decoration by Hungarian architect Frigyes Feszl. On its inauguration day, Franz Liszt and Camille Saint-Saëns played the organ — it's one of the rare synagogues to have one. During the Second World War the building was used as a German radio base and then as a stable. Book tickets in advance during peak season — the queue can be long. The ticket includes the Hungarian Jewish Museum and Raoul Wallenberg Park. Allow at least an hour and a half to see everything. Men must cover their heads (kippas provided at the entrance). The synagogue is closed on Saturdays.

visits.text: Hungarian State Opera House

activities.text: Architect Miklós Ybl spent nine years on this building, from 1875 to 1884, with a stated ambition: to surpass the Vienna Opera in elegance, if not in size (1,261 seats against Vienna's 1,709). Emperor Franz Joseph financed the construction, and according to legend, a ticket for the opening night on 27 September 1884 cost the equivalent of two horses. The neo-Renaissance façade is crowned by statues of Franz Liszt and Ferenc Erkel (composer of the Hungarian national anthem) at the main entrance, and sculpted portraits of Mozart, Beethoven, Verdi and other musical giants along the roofline. Inside, 7 kg of gold were used in the auditorium's decoration, and the acoustics rank among the finest in Europe — slightly ahead of the Palais Garnier, according to a Hungarian university study. Gustav Mahler served as music director here from 1888 to 1891. Book the guided tour in advance, especially in summer: it's one of the best in Budapest.

visits.text: Vajdahunyad Castle
Városliget Park

activities.text: Here's a place that always surprises: this castle that looks straight out of the Middle Ages was actually built in 1896, from wood and cardboard, for the National Millennium Exhibition celebrating 1,000 years since the Magyar conquest of the Carpathian Basin. The Hungarian public fell so in love with it that they refused to see it demolished — so it was rebuilt in stone and brick between 1904 and 1908. Emperor Franz Joseph himself came to inaugurate it in 1907. Architect Ignác Alpár chose to blend Romanesque, Gothic, Renaissance and baroque in a single complex, representing the evolution of Hungarian architecture across ten centuries. The Gothic section is modelled directly on Vajdahunyad Castle in Transylvania (now in Romania), where legend places the imprisonment of Vlad Dracula. In the courtyard, don't miss the statue of the Anonymous, the masked chronicler at the court of King Béla III. A bust of Béla Lugosi (the Hungarian actor who became Count Dracula in Hollywood) has also been installed in a niche. In winter, the artificial lake becomes the largest open-air ice rink in Europe.

visits.text: Széchenyi Thermal Baths

activities.text: The big mustard-yellow building in Városliget park is the largest thermal complex in Europe. Designed by architect Győző Czigler in a neo-Baroque style, it was inaugurated on 16 June 1913 — more than 200,000 visitors came in the first year alone. The water rises from an artesian well drilled in 1879 to a depth of 1,246 metres, at a temperature of 76°C. It's rich in calcium, magnesium, sulphates and fluorides, and renowned for its effects on joint pain. The outdoor pools — three large basins between 27 and 38°C — have become one of Budapest's most iconic images: chess players facing off in the hot water even in the middle of winter. Indoors: 15 additional pools, saunas, steam rooms. Arrive early in the morning or on a weekday to avoid the crowds. Bring swimwear, a towel and flip-flops. If visiting in summer, book your ticket in advance.

visits.text: Margaret Island (Margit-sziget)
Dominican convent ruins

activities.text: Margaret Island (2.5 km long, 500 m wide, 100 hectares) takes its name from a true story: the daughter of King Béla IV, Margaret, lived in a Dominican convent here from childhood until her death in 1271, fulfilling her father's vow to dedicate her if he saved Hungary from the Mongol invasion of 1242. She was canonised in 1943 by Pope Pius XII. The ruins of that convent are still visible at the heart of the island. In 1914, when a storm toppled a tree, the bell of the St Michael monastery was found among its roots — cast in the 15th century, it is considered one of the oldest bells in Hungary. The island is free to enter — cars are not allowed. At the southern end, the musical fountain puts on a sound-and-light show every hour from 10 AM to 10 PM in summer. Bring a bicycle or comfortable shoes: the running track circles the entire island for 5.3 km.

visits.text: House of Terror (Terror Háza)

activities.text: This building, erected in 1880 at 60 Andrássy Avenue, has known two dark lives: from 1944 to 1945 it was the headquarters of the fascist Arrow Cross Party; from 1945 to 1956 it became the base of the ÁVH, the communist secret police — Hungary's equivalent of the KGB. Hundreds of people were interrogated, tortured and imprisoned here. Opened in 2002, the Terror Háza museum retraces both totalitarian occupations across some thirty rooms. The visit starts on the second floor with the Arrow Cross era, then devotes 90% of the space to the communist regime. The culmination: the descent into the basement, where the prisoner cells were. The exterior of the building was designed so that the word 'TERROR' casts its shadow onto the façade. Take the audioguide (available in English and French) — without it, the explanations are very limited. Arrive at opening time to avoid school groups. The visit easily takes two to three hours.

visits.text: Andrássy Avenue
Heroes' Square

activities.text: Andrássy Avenue stretches exactly 2,310 metres, linking the centre of Pest to Városliget park. Construction began in 1871 at the initiative of Count Gyula Andrássy, and the avenue was officially inaugurated on 20 August 1876, though the last buildings were not completed until 1885. It was inscribed on the UNESCO World Heritage list in 2002, notably for its remarkably cohesive neo-Renaissance façades — and because beneath it runs the M1 metro line, inaugurated in 1896 and the first electrified underground railway on the European continent. The avenue has gone through many names: Sugárút, then Andrássy út in 1885, then Sztálin út in 1950, then back to its original name from 1990. Walk from the Opera to Heroes' Square: the first three blocks are the densest in aristocratic mansions, the second section opens onto townhouses with plane-tree-shaded service lanes, and the final stretch leads to the villas. At the far end, Heroes' Square and its statues of the seven Magyar tribal chieftains who founded Hungary is one of Budapest's great esplanades.

visits.text: St Stephen's Basilica

activities.text: Construction of this basilica took 54 years — from 1851 to 1905 — and involved three successive architects, including Miklós Ybl (the same as for the Opera), who redesigned the dome after its catastrophic collapse on 22 January 1868. Its height of 96 metres is deliberately symbolic: it exactly matches that of the Hungarian Parliament, representing the balance between spiritual and secular power, and recalls the year 896, when the Magyars settled in the Carpathian Basin. Inside, more than 50 different types of marble adorn the walls. The Chapel of the Holy Right houses the country's most sacred relic: the mummified right hand of King Saint Stephen, Hungary's first Christian king (crowned in 1000, died in 1038, canonised in 1083). To see the relic up close, insert a coin into the lighting mechanism. Every 20 August (Hungarian National Day), it is carried in procession around the building. Don't miss the climb to the dome for a 360° panoramic view over Budapest — take the lift halfway, then finish on foot.

visits.text: Stroll along the Danube embankment

activities.text: The Danube embankment on the Pest side, between the Chain Bridge and Elisabeth Bridge, is a UNESCO World Heritage Site. This is where you best understand why Budapest is nicknamed the Pearl of the Danube. An evening river cruise offers the most striking perspectives on the illuminated Parliament, Buda Castle and its gardens, the Fisherman's Bastion and six successive bridges. Most cruises last about an hour and include drinks and commentary. Operators offer dinner packages or simply panoramic options depending on your budget. If you prefer to stay on land, find a spot on the Pest embankment an hour before sunset: the golden light on the Buda façades, then the gradual illumination of the Parliament as night falls, is a free and unforgettable spectacle.

visits.text: Gellért Baths (temporarily closed for renovation)
Gellért Hotel and baths exterior

activities.text: Please note: the Gellért Baths have been closed for renovation since October 2025 and are expected to reopen in 2028. You can nevertheless admire the exterior of the building, which is a work of art in its own right. Construction was entrusted to architects Ármin Hegedűs, Artúr Sebestyén and Izidor Sterk, and the baths opened in 1918 in a magnificent Art Nouveau (Hungarian Secession) style. The grand interior hall is crowned by a coloured glass roof, the pools are surrounded by columns and decorated with ceramics from the Zsolnay manufactory — the same as for the Great Market Hall and Matthias Church. The adjacent Gellért Hotel, set against the hill of the same name, is one of the most beautiful architectural complexes in Budapest. While waiting for the reopening, enjoy a walk at the foot of Gellért Hill and the nearby Liberty Bridge, and try the alternative baths: the Rudas Baths, five minutes on foot, are less touristy and historically just as interesting.

visits.text: Fisherman's Bastion

activities.text: This monument that looks like a medieval fortress was in fact never used for defence: it was designed from the outset as a panoramic terrace, built between 1895 and 1902 by architect Frigyes Schulek to commemorate the millennium of the arrival of the Magyar tribes in 895. The seven turrets with their conical roofs each represent one of the seven tribal chieftains who followed Prince Árpád into Hungary — the roof shapes deliberately echo the nomadic tents of the steppes. Its name comes from the fishing village that once stood at the foot of the hill in the Middle Ages, where the fishermen were charged with defending this stretch of the ramparts. The view of the Parliament from the bastion is the finest in Budapest — especially at sunset and dawn, when the golden light grazes the Parliament's white façade from across the Danube. The lower terraces are free; the upper turrets require a ticket during the day (free entry before 9 AM and after 9 PM in summer). In front of the building stands the equestrian statue of King Saint Stephen, by sculptor Alajos Stróbl.

visits.text: Matthias Church

activities.text: Founded in 1255 by King Béla IV, converted into a mosque by the Ottomans in 1541, rebuilt in baroque style by the Habsburgs after 1686, then restored to its neo-Gothic appearance by architect Frigyes Schulek between 1873 and 1896: Matthias Church has survived every regime. It takes its popular name from King Matthias Corvinus (1458–1490), who celebrated two marriages here and added the Matthias Tower (60 metres), completed in 1470. On 8 June 1867, Emperor Franz Joseph I and his wife Elisabeth of Wittelsbach (Sisi) were crowned King and Queen of Hungary here — Franz Liszt conducted the premiere of his Coronation Mass for the occasion. The roof of multicoloured glazed Zsolnay tiles is the building's most recognisable visual signature. Inside, the Art Nouveau frescoes covering every wall and column were repainted during the restoration. Classical music concerts are held regularly — the acoustics are excellent.

visits.text: Gellért Hill and Citadel
Liberty Statue

activities.text: Gellért Hill rises to 235 metres, making it the highest vantage point within central Budapest. It takes its name from the Venetian Bishop Gerard (Gellért in Hungarian), invited to Hungary by Saint Stephen to evangelise the country — legend has it that he was hurled into the Danube from this hill in a nail-studded barrel during a peasant revolt in 1046. The Citadel was built in 1851 by the Habsburgs as a symbol of authority over a Hungary that had just lost its war of independence in 1848–49. In 1897 the Austrian army was permanently evacuated. The Liberty Statue, inaugurated in 1947 by the Soviets to commemorate the liberation of Budapest, depicts a 14-metre woman holding a palm above her head (40 metres including the pedestal). In 1990, with the fall of the communist regime, the Soviet soldiers that once flanked her were sent to the Memento Park. Walk up from Liberty Bridge or Elisabeth Bridge — allow 30 to 40 minutes. The 360° view over Budapest is the most complete in the city.

visits.text: Hungarian Parliament (Országház)

activities.text: The Hungarian Parliament is the third-largest parliamentary building in the world, behind those of Romania and Argentina. Its construction, which employed roughly 100,000 workers, 40 million bricks and 40 kg of gold for decoration, ran from 1885 to 1902 to the designs of Imre Steindl — who lost his sight before seeing his masterpiece completed. The building measures 268 metres long and 123 metres wide, contains 691 rooms, 29 staircases, 10 interior courtyards and a dome 96 metres high (a figure chosen in reference to the year 896, the founding of Hungary). This 96 is also the height of St Stephen's Basilica across the river — the two tallest buildings in Budapest are deliberately balanced. Beneath the dome, guarded day and night by soldiers in dress uniform, lies the Crown of Saint Stephen, the most precious treasure of the Hungarian nation. The changing of the guard is highly choreographed and worth catching. Visits are guided only and run in several languages. Book in advance: morning slots go first.

map_keywords: Magyar Nemzeti Galéria Budapest Budavári Palota

visits.text: Buda Castle (Royal Palace)
Hungarian National Gallery

activities.text: To reach the castle, take the Budavári Sikló funicular from Clark Ádám Square, directly opposite the Chain Bridge. In service since 1870, it's the oldest form of public transport in Budapest — it was destroyed during the war and faithfully rebuilt. The 95-metre ride climbs 50 metres in a few minutes and offers a striking view over the Danube banks. At the top, Buda Castle has commanded the city since the 13th century. A residence of Hungarian kings through to the end of Habsburg rule, heavily damaged during the Siege of Budapest (1944–1945), it was restored in the second half of the 20th century. The site has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 1987. Today it houses the Hungarian National Gallery (Hungarian art from the 11th century to the present — a must for fans of the Nagybánya School and Expressionism), the Budapest History Museum and the Széchenyi National Library. The outdoor esplanade is freely accessible at all times — the gardens offer one of the finest views over Pest and the Danube.

seo.h1_title: What to See in Budapest: Architecture and Heritage
seo.intro_paragraph: Budapest packs into a single visit what few capitals can rival: the haunting cast-iron shoes on the Danube bank, the splendour of the Great Synagogue built between 1854 and 1859, the neo-Baroque magnificence of the Széchenyi Baths, and the royal panoramas from the Fisherman's Bastion. Nineteen stops spanning ten centuries of Hungarian history, from the lanes of the Jewish quarter to the gilded halls of the Parliament.
seo.seo_hook: Danube shoes, Moorish synagogue, neo-Baroque baths, Gothic Parliament: Budapest — memory and grandeur on the river.
featured_list: Hungarian Parliament: 268 metres of neo-Gothic façade on the Danube
Fisherman's Bastion: a panoramic terrace with 7 symbolic turrets, completed in 1902
Great Synagogue: the largest in Europe, inaugurated in 1859 with Franz Liszt at the organ
State Opera: Miklós Ybl's neo-Renaissance masterpiece, with acoustics among the finest in Europe

places_taglines.HU::shoe-memorial: Sixty pairs of cast-iron shoes honouring the Jews shot on the Danube bank in 1944–1945
places_taglines.HU::great-market-hall: A 10,000 m² neo-Gothic hall inaugurated in 1897, crowned with Zsolnay ceramic tiles
places_taglines.HU::memory-garden: A weeping willow in metal bearing the names of Holocaust victims, inside the former ghetto walls
places_taglines.HU::jewish-quarter: Budapest's 7th District, where synagogues and ruin bars share Art Nouveau courtyards
places_taglines.HU::great-synagogue: Europe's largest synagogue — 3,500 seats, Moorish style, inaugurated in 1859 on Dohány Street
places_taglines.HU::state-opera: A neo-Renaissance opera house from 1884 by Miklós Ybl, with 7 kg of gold in its auditorium
places_taglines.HU::vajdahunyad-castle: An eclectic castle built in 1896 for the Millennium, rebuilt in stone between 1904 and 1908
places_taglines.HU::szechenyi-baths: Neo-Baroque baths from 1913 — Pest's first thermal complex, where chess games unfold in outdoor pools
places_taglines.HU::margaret-island: A 100-hectare Danube island with 13th-century Dominican convent ruins and free admission
places_taglines.HU::house-of-terror: Former headquarters of the Arrow Cross and then the communist secret police, now a museum since 2002
places_taglines.HU::andrassy-avenue: A 2,310-metre UNESCO boulevard inaugurated in 1876, with continental Europe's first metro line beneath it
places_taglines.HU::saint-stephen-basilica: A 96-metre neo-Renaissance basilica completed in 1905, housing Saint Stephen's mummified hand
places_taglines.HU::danube-cruise: A UNESCO-listed river promenade between the Chain Bridge and Elisabeth Bridge
places_taglines.HU::gellert-baths: An Art Nouveau masterpiece from 1918, closed for renovation until 2028, with Zsolnay ceramics and glass canopies
places_taglines.HU::fishermans-bastion: A neo-Romanesque panoramic terrace from 1902, with 7 turrets for the 7 founding tribes and a Parliament view
places_taglines.HU::matthias-church: A coronation church founded in 1255, roofed in Zsolnay tiles, where Liszt conducted his Coronation Mass in 1867
places_taglines.HU::gellert-hill: A 235-metre hill topped by the 1851 Habsburg Citadel and the 1947 Liberty Statue
places_taglines.HU::parliament: The world's third-largest parliament — 268 metres, 691 rooms, the Crown of Saint Stephen beneath the dome
places_taglines.HU::buda-castle: A UNESCO-listed royal palace since 1987, home to the national gallery and the 1870 funicular

highlights: Great Synagogue on Dohány Street: the largest synagogue in Europe, seating 3,500, inaugurated in 1859
Hungarian Parliament: 268 metres of neo-Gothic façade on the Danube, 691 rooms and 40 kg of gold
Széchenyi Baths: Pest's first thermal complex, opened in 1913, famous for chess games in the pools
Vajdahunyad Castle: 4 architectural styles in one building, constructed for the 1896 Millennium
